Delay Code
Advisor
1
Process
Select which part of the turnaround the delay occurred in — boarding, fuelling, catering, etc.
2
Reason
Choose the specific cause — late arrival, missing documentation, equipment failure, and so on.
3
Stakeholder
Indicate who is responsible — airline, ground handler, crew, ATC, or another party.
Always select the true root cause. Accurate coding drives better decisions — for your flight, your team, and the whole operation.
Menu
IATA · AHM 732
A smarter way to code delays

Three letters. One root cause. Complete clarity.

What is AHM 732?

AHM 732 replaces the old numeric delay code system. Designed to be fast, clear, and accurate — so the person assigning the code can do it while the delay is still fresh in mind.

How the code is built

Every delay code is exactly three characters. Each answers one question.

1 Process Where in the turnaround?
2 Reason What happened?
3 Stakeholder Who was responsible?
Example

Cleaning arrives understaffed and delays boarding:

K Process Cleaning
T Reason Staff lack / late
N Stakeholder Service provider
KTN
The 7 process groups

Processes are organised in 7 groups that follow the aircraft through a turnaround. They form the first character of the code.

A · Arrival
B · Ramp & Load
C · Servicing
D · Crew & Docs
E · Pax Handling
F · Disruption
G · Network
Designed for real-time use

AHM 732 works best on portable devices. Assign the code while the delay is fresh — accurate coding supports better decisions for your flight, your team, and the whole operation.

AHM 732 © IATA — Airport Handling Manual, 42nd edition Official IATA tool ↗
AHM 732 · Character 3
Who was responsible?

The most contested character. Always pick the true root cause — not the most convenient party.

The stakeholder character identifies who was operationally responsible for the root cause of the delay — not who was first on scene, not who filed the report, and not whichever party is easiest to name.

Under time pressure it is easy to pick the most visible party or the one with least pushback. That defeats the purpose of the code.

✓Choose the party whose action or inaction directly caused the delay.
✗Do not choose the party that was standing nearby.
✗Do not choose the party that reported the delay.
✗Do not choose the party that is easiest to blame.

Accurate stakeholder coding is the foundation of meaningful delay analysis. A code entered under time pressure and later disputed costs far more than the 30 seconds it takes to get it right.

Ask yourself: "If this went to a review, could I defend this stakeholder choice with facts?" If not — reconsider.
Airline
AAirline — general

A commercial or operational airline decision not clearly attributable to a specific department, crew, or external party. Use when the delay originates from within the airline but cannot be pinpointed further.

Commercial hold decision · Airline-wide IT failure
BFlight deck crew / entire crew

The pilots or entire crew caused the delay — through late arrival at the aircraft, missing or invalid documents, crew duty time limitations being reached, or extended pre-departure procedures.

Captain arrives late · Crew duty time reached · Missing pilot documents
CCabin crew

Cabin crew specifically caused the delay — safety checks not completed in time, crew not ready for boarding to commence, or a crew error or request outside normal operational requirements.

Cabin safety check delays boarding · Crew not in position at boarding start
D / E / F / G / H — Airline departments

When the cause sits within a specific airline department, the code is more precise than A.

DCrew scheduling
EGround ops
FCommercial
GOps control
HEngineering
Crew rostered incorrectly → D · OCC late departure clearance → G · Maintenance sign-off delayed → H
Ground handling
KGround handler

The contracted ground handling company was operationally responsible — through a late start, insufficient staff, equipment unavailability, or a handling error during the turnaround.

Use K if the handler caused it, even if airline crew were present and noticed it first. Presence does not equal responsibility.
Handler starts loading late · Insufficient check-in staff · Baggage belt breakdown
NService provider

A third-party service provider who is not the ground handler caused the delay. This includes catering companies, cleaning contractors, fuelling suppliers, de-icing operators, or other external service parties.

Catering truck late · Cleaning not completed · Fuel bowser unavailable
External parties
IGovernmental authorities

A governmental body acting within its authority caused the delay — customs, immigration, border control, health authorities, or police. Use when their decision or action directly held the flight.

Immigration holds passenger · Customs inspection of cargo · Police called to aircraft
JPassenger

Use only when the passenger's own action or inaction directly caused the delay — a late show, no-show, disruptive behaviour, or refusal to board. Not to be used if the airline or handler mishandled the passenger situation.

Do not use J because a passenger was involved. Use J only when their own behaviour was the direct cause.
Passenger no-show · Late to gate · Disruptive behaviour requiring removal
O / P / Q — ATFM  ·  R / S / T — Local ATC

Air traffic flow management and ATC restrictions are externally imposed and non-discretionary. Use these when a regulation, slot, or ATC instruction — not an airline or handler decision — caused the delay.

ATFM departure slot → O  ·  En-route regulation → P  ·  ATC late start-up clearance → R
Airport & unknown
XAirport

The airport infrastructure, facilities, or airport authority caused the delay — through gate unavailability, runway or taxiway closure, insufficient fire cover, or construction works.

Gate not available · Runway closure · Insufficient fire cover at apron
ZNot attributable

Use only when the cause genuinely cannot be attributed to any party — extraordinary events, unknown causes at time of coding, or delays falling within the permissible tolerance window. Avoid as a default when attribution is uncertain. Investigate first.

Delay within tolerance window · Extraordinary event with no clear party
Always the root cause

Accurate stakeholder coding drives accountability, better handler contracts, and smarter operational decisions. A code assigned under time pressure and later challenged is far more costly than the 30 seconds it takes to get it right.

Always select the true root cause — not the most convenient party
